content:
Shift Impact — N/A (this is the point)
Zero shifts this week — full vacation, the no-shift baseline test for the sleep drift question. Verdict: drift persisted without shift pressure. Bedtimes went 01:44 → 22:51 → 03:22 → 02:58 → 03:25 → 04:48 → 01:29. That's not shift-confounded circadian delay, and it's not "recovering from work" either — it got progressively worse as the week went on with nothing external forcing it. This points more toward the psychological pull (evening time feels more valuable) than the light/circadian explanation, since a pure light effect wouldn't have gotten worse day 5-6 into a rest week. Worth locking in the soft ceiling (~00:30) as the next move rather than waiting for more data — you have your answer.
Training — basically a rest week
One real session all week: July 2's mixed walk/run block. The run segment (1.74km) landed 47% Z3 / 48% Z4 with an elevation gain of 65m in under 2km — that's a steep short segment, and it's the same pattern as before: terrain overrides Z2 intent immediately. Load is dropping fast (297→168 over the week, ACWR 1.2→0.6), which is fine and expected for a deliberate rest week, but it does mean you're bleeding some training effect if this stretches longer. Steps cratered too — 325 on July 2, sub-2,500 average for the week. This is recovery-week territory, not a Z2-habit-building week, so don't read anything into the Z2 failure here beyond "same structural issue, small sample."
Nutrition — no pattern, no adherence
This is the sloppiest nutrition week you've logged in a while. Calories ranged 1010–2657, protein 55–171g, with no day landing near your 1600/120g targets. Some of that is legitimate vacation variance, but two multi-hundred-kcal swings back to back (1010 → 2246 → 1436) with matching protein swings (100 → 102 → 55) suggests inconsistent logging more than intentional flexible eating. I'd flag this against the memory note about portion-estimation gaps driving the weight plateau — this week is exhibit A. Hard to trust the weight trend as "real" gain vs. "under-logged intake finally catching up" when the food data itself is this noisy.
Recovery — HRV/RHR good, everything else says you're under-recovering from sleep, not training
HRV held rock steady (69–75, no negative trend) and RHR actually improved across the week (48→44). Those numbers alone look great. But sleep score fell hard (76→55) and recovery hours cratered (79.9h→1.2h) by the end — that's the Garmin recovery-time estimate collapsing, driven by declining sleep quality (REM dropped from 1.5h to 0.1h on the last night logged, deep sleep also thinning). The readiness score climbing to 54 by day 7 is a bit of a red herring here — it's weighted toward HRV/RHR, which stayed fine, and isn't picking up on the sleep architecture degrading underneath it. I'd trust the sleep and recovery-hours numbers over the readiness score this week.
Key Takeaway
The sleep baseline test gave you a real answer: the drift isn't shift-driven, it's happening on its own even with total schedule freedom, and it got worse as the week went on. That's your signal to introduce the ~00:30 soft ceiling now rather than waiting. Separately, this week's nutrition logging was too noisy to draw any real conclusion about the weight trend — before blaming the scale, get back to logging consistently for a week and see if the "rise" holds up against actual data instead of gaps.

Weekly Review: Jørgen | June 14–20, 2026
1. Shift Impact
A busy mixed week — 2 day shifts (06-17, 06-18), 3 evening shifts (06-15, 06-16, 06-20), 2 days off. The day-shift block hit hardest: June 18's sleep score was the week's joint-worst at 58 (5h19, bed 00:55→wake 06:18), and body battery on that day only reached 85 at peak — the lowest peak of the week. June 16 also scored 58 despite being an evening-shift day, showing the strain wasn't purely shift-type driven — late bedtimes (04:44, 04:05) were a recurring theme regardless of shift. The one genuine reset came June 19 (a day off): 12h17 of sleep, sleep score 83, HRV steady at 69, body battery peaked at 100. That's a clear compensatory crash after the accumulated debt from June 15–18, not a random good night.
2. Training
Heavier and harder than the following week. Two tempo runs: June 15 (6.39km, HR164 avg, 53% Z4) and June 17 (4.87km, HR173 avg, 90% Z4 — essentially an all-out effort, max HR 195, hitting your ceiling). Both were run at significantly higher intensity than Zone 2 target (117-137), continuing the pattern of zone 3-4 dominance over true aerobic base work. June 20 added two short bike sessions (6.36km + 6.42km, mixed Z2-Z3). Load climbed through the week — ACWR hit 1.6 on both June 15 and June 17, signaling acute load running well ahead of chronic — and June 17's run in particular, on the first of two day shifts, was a hard push on top of a high-stress schedule day. This was the highest-load week of the period; not unsafe, but a clear signal that more genuine Zone 2 work (rather than tempo-paced "easy" runs) was needed going forward.
3. Nutrition
The standout problem day is June 18: 3,102.9 kcal with only 85.5g protein — the week's highest calorie day and second-lowest protein day, landing right after a rough night (sleep score 58) and a day shift. That's a textbook fatigue-and-stress eating pattern. June 14 has no food log at all. Protein hit a genuine high only on June 17 (164.9g) and June 20 (145g); most other days sat well below the 150g target, including a low of 74.6g on June 19 — the rest day, ironically, when there was no excuse for under-fueling. Average protein across logged days sits meaningfully short of target, and the variance (74.6g to 217g range across recent weeks) is the bigger issue than any single day.
4. Recovery
HRV held in a reasonably tight, stable band (65–76) with no crash trend — a genuinely positive sign given the load. RHR sat in a healthy 42–46 range, improving slightly by week's end. Readiness, however, was weak for most of the week — LOW (34-40) across June 15-18, only climbing to MODERATE (50-51) on the two lower-stress days (June 14, 19, 20). That LOW stretch lines up exactly with the shift-and-tempo-run cluster: hard training stacked on disrupted sleep, with the body clearly flagging it before the June 19 reset.
5. Key Takeaway
Two tempo-paced runs in a high-shift-density week was too much load stacked on too little recovery. Both June 15 and June 17 pushed HR well into Z3-Z4 territory (164 and 173 avg) instead of true Zone 2, and June 17's effort specifically landed on a day-shift day — exactly the "hard run on a hard day" pattern to avoid. Readiness stayed LOW for four straight days as a direct result. Going forward, treat tempo-paced running as a deliberate, occasional choice rather than the default "easy run" pace, and reserve hard efforts for days with genuinely supportive readiness and recovery — not just whenever a run happens to be scheduled.
6. Body Composition / Trend
Daily weigh-ins this week: 76.76kg (Jun 14) → 77.04kg (Jun 15) → 76.44kg (Jun 16) → 75.93kg (Jun 17) → 75.68kg (Jun 18) → 75.37kg (Jun 19) → 76.19kg (Jun 20). A small bump early in the week (likely post-tempo-run water retention/inflammation on Jun 15) followed by a steady decline to the week's low of 75.37kg on Jun 19, then a slight rebound. Body fat tracked similarly: peaked at 25.2% (Jun 18) before dropping to a weekly low of 23.9% (Jun 19) — consistent with the big sleep/recovery reset that day. Muscle mass held essentially flat (32.19–32.59kg) throughout, no concerning loss despite the deficit.

Weekly Review: Jørgen | June 21–27, 2026
1. Shift Impact
One day shift (June 21), then six vacation days with no shift constraint. June 21's sleep score was the week's worst at 47 (4h22, bed 01:32→wake 05:55), driven by the early 07:30 start. From June 22 onward, sleep scores climbed steadily — 51, 60, 80, 70, 76, and finally 86 on June 27. Bedtime drifted progressively later through the week (01:32 → 02:05 → 05:29 → 03:36 → 01:31 → 03:52 → 03:39) — entirely without shift pressure. This settles the open question from a couple weeks back: the drift isn't shift-driven, it's a vacation/light/value-driven pattern. A soft bedtime ceiling (~00:30) is the right next experiment rather than a hard sleep-by rule.
2. Training
Light through most of the week, then one big effort. June 21: 6.3km run, HR133 avg, 73% Zone 2 — clean execution. June 22, 24, 25, 26 were rest days. June 23 had a short 2km walk and an easy indoor bike session (HR119, mostly Z2). June 27: a 49.2km tempo ride, 2h07, HR157 avg, 59% Zone 4 — the week's dominant session, load 285. This rode a genuine PRIME-readiness window and was a well-grounded call given cycling's low Achilles risk. Acute load (397) now sits above chronic load (322), ACWR 1.2 — expected after a single hard session, not a trend.
3. Nutrition
Highly variable, vacation-typical. June 22 is the clear outlier: 3,661 kcal, 551.9g carbs, 269.8g sugar, only 103.1g protein — no training to justify it. June 25 and 26 sat well under target (1,658 and 1,470 kcal). June 27 (tempo day) logged 2,713 kcal with 401.7g carbs and 107.8g protein — consistent with the big-training-day rule of eating back roughly a quarter to a third of the burn, weighted to carbs. Best protein days: June 21 (217.4g, on a shift day) and June 26 (175.7g). No day hit both calorie and protein targets together — the week reads as reactive eating rather than planned intake.
4. Recovery
HRV trended upward across the week — 72 → 71 → 69 → 74 → 69 → 74 → 76 — peaking on June 27, right before the tempo effort. RHR stayed in a tight, healthy band (43–47, avg 44). Stress climbed slightly toward the back half (21 → 37) alongside the harder ride. Readiness swung widely and ended the week on its worst point — averaging 40.4 across the week but dropping from 32 (LOW) on June 21 to 13 (POOR) by June 27, a net -19 over the week. Recovery time jumped to 96h after the tempo ride. That end-of-week crash is the system working as intended after a hard effort, not a concerning trend.
5. Key Takeaway
The sleep-drift question is answered — it's a free-time/light pattern, not a shift problem. Bedtime drifted later through a week with zero shift pressure. Combined with the 96h recovery window opened by Saturday's tempo ride, the days ahead are a good stretch for something other than running — Day B (bodyweight strength) is still untouched and fits well here, being low-impact on both the Achilles and the cycling recovery.
6. Body Composition / Trend
Daily weigh-ins this week: 75.89kg (Jun 22) → 76.46kg (Jun 23) → 75.81kg (Jun 24) → 76.3kg (Jun 25) → 76.04kg (Jun 26) → 75.55kg (Jun 27). No weigh-in logged June 21. Noisy day-to-day (normal — hydration, food timing, the tempo ride's effect on water%), but the trend across the week is flat-to-slightly-down, ending at the week's lowest reading (75.55kg) alongside the week's lowest body fat (24.4%, down from 24.5–25.2% mid-week).
